artifacts.content:
Hi Izzy,

I’m hiding in the bedroom while the kids argue over which “Nailed It!” episode to rewatch, so I figured I’d actually write instead of just sending you half‑coherent texts.

Life feels like it’s all riverfront, all the time. I spent most of today tweaking the project description again – making sure it still clearly says “North Riverfront Mixed-Use Redevelopment Project” with the 285 units, 63,500 sf of commercial, 48,000 sf of office, riverfront promenade, 1.5‑acre park, the whole EMU-to-Riverfront Mixed Use GP amendment and MU-RF rezone package – but without accidentally slipping in any of our family drama that’s been shaping how I think about it.

I keep catching myself almost typing things like “after Mateo was nearly hit in the Northside crosswalk on March 3, 2023” when I’m describing the new signal at E. Riverfront and 5th and the traffic calming by the school. Or wanting to write about Elena’s October 2024 IEP safety meeting when we talked about using that 14‑foot path from the park to the L Street back gate as an evacuation route for kids with mobility issues. All of that is why I’m so intense about the design, but it can’t live in the formal language.

Same with the affordable piece. Every time I see “approximately 43 deed‑restricted units (15%)” I think about how close Miguel came to losing his Main Street shop during the 2020 shutdowns and crashing here at 250 Civic Center Plaza. Or Mom’s fall on that dark dirt path by the river in October 2022 when she was walking Elena and Mateo home from Northside – that’s why I pushed so hard for the fenced pocket park and a lit, paved connection behind the mixed‑use building.

Anyway, kids need snacks and Daniel’s grading DBQs again. Call this weekend?

Besos,
Amanda

Miguel,

I’ve been thinking about you a lot since we went over that relocation plan draft on January 10. Seeing Riverbend Court spelled out – 24 units on 5th Street, your 2B on that month‑to‑month at $1,050 – and then those projected $300–$400 rent jumps if folks get pushed to newer complexes farther from the levee just made my stomach drop. I know you’re still digging out from the February 2023 wage garnishment on that old credit card judgment, and the idea of your housing costs jumping like that on top of everything at the shop is… a lot.

It doesn’t help that your auto shop keeps orbiting my work in other ways. I still remember the March 2022 solvent spill – what was it, around 120 gallons? – and all the soil and groundwater sampling they made you do for six months. Now, every time I’m reviewing the contaminated soils and hazardous materials sections for the North Riverfront Draft EIR (yes, it’s officially a Draft EIR, not a Mit Neg Dec), I hear your voice on the phone from that day, panicking about the drainage ditch.

I’m trying to make sure the project’s voluntary relocation assistance program for low‑income tenants is actually meaningful, not just window dressing. We’ve been talking about up to 36 months of rent differential in some cases, and I keep picturing you and some of the Riverbend Court folks when I argue for stronger protections.

Let’s sit down soon – maybe at the taco truck on E. Riverfront – and go over your budget and some backup options. I don’t want you blindsided if the building changes hands.

Love,
Amanda

Planner’s personal notebook – Northside / riverfront threads

I need one place to brain‑dump how much of this work is tangled up with family stuff so I can keep it out of the official files and still be honest with myself.

Northside Elementary keeps showing up everywhere. Elena’s asthma flare during that October 2023 air‑quality incident by the playground, when the diesel truck storage yard west of the campus was idling forever, is why I can’t stop staring at the zoning map that still shows that M‑1/EMU strip within ~300 feet of the school’s western fence. Present land use is technically “light industrial,” but in my head it’s “the place that made my kid wheeze.” After the November 2, 2023 meeting with Principal Karen Lopez, I wrote “UR buffer” in the margin three times.

Then there’s the May 3, 2023 air quality workshop in the Northside multipurpose room. Parents from the J Street apartments talking about their kids’ asthma, two ambulance trips to Riverview General in March 2023 on a high‑ozone day, and me sitting there thinking, “Mateo is in Ms. Ramirez’s 2nd‑grade class with these kids.” No wonder everyone is terrified about diesel backup generators and the concrete batch plant within 500 feet of the yard.

Layer on top of that the emergency‑access memo from October 2023 about the Levee Road detour adding up to 6 minutes to ambulance times, right after Mom’s minor stroke on October 19 near Jefferson Blvd and the rush to Sutter Medical Center. Of course I’m hypersensitive.

Reminder to self: document the issues rigorously, but don’t let my family become the case studies in the CEQA chapters.

Hi Pastor Jorge,

I hope you’re doing well and that the last mobile pantry day went smoothly. I’ve been revising the community center site plan again and wanted to confirm a few details we discussed back on March 18, 2024.

We’ve kept the unmarked rear service entrance and screened loading zone on the plans exactly where you requested, with enough space for the truck to back in and unload without being visible from the main plaza. On paper it’s just labeled “service loading area,” but the intent is still very much to accommodate the monthly food pantry your Riverview First Baptist congregation has been operating since Spring 2022. I haven’t forgotten that you’re serving about 60 undocumented Northside families each month – I just know we need to be discreet so people feel safe coming.

At the same time, I’m trying to balance a lot of other community sensitivities. After the January 2023 drowning of 7‑year‑old Luis Martinez near the informal riverfront trailhead, the Northside PTA asked us to incorporate a small memorial garden and quiet seating area in the new neighborhood park. We’ve tucked that “contemplation garden” into the park program, along with child‑safety and river education signage, without calling explicit attention to the incident in the public materials.

My mom’s fall and hip fracture on that unlit dirt path along the river in October 2022, when she was walking my kids home from Northside, is also in the back of my mind. It’s part of why I pushed so hard for the small fenced pocket park and a properly lit walkway behind the mixed‑use building.

I’d love to walk the site with you again in a few weeks to make sure the design still works for your logistics.

Best,
Amanda

metadata.subject: Community center service entrance and park details

artifacts.content:
Event: Morning bike commute w/ kids – post‑DUI routine
Date: 2024-09-09 (recurring weekdays)
Time: 7:15 AM – 8:10 AM
Location: 250 Civic Center Plaza → Northside Elementary → City Hall
Attendees: Amanda, Elena, Mateo

Notes:

Leave the house by 7:15 so we’re not sprinting. Ever since Daniel’s DUI arrest on March 18, 2023 and the 18‑month license suspension that kicked in last April, this has been our normal: I bike with the kids from 250 Civic Center Plaza to Northside, drop them at the gate around 7:40, then continue on to the Community Development office.

Remember to pack Elena’s soccer gear in the pannier on Tuesdays and Mateo’s piano book on Thursdays. Helmet check before we roll – especially for Mateo, who still forgets the strap. This route actually isn’t bad: we cut over toward E. Riverfront Drive, pass the aging industrial parcels that are turning into my 18.4‑acre North Riverfront Mixed‑Use Redevelopment Project (285 units, 63,500 sf commercial, 48,000 sf office, riverfront promenade, central plaza, 1.5‑acre park), then loop back to Civic Center Plaza.

Also trying to keep up the walking habit Dr. Nguyen pushed after my May 2, 2023 physical (LDL 192, “lose at least 15 pounds”). On days when I don’t have early meetings, I may walk the 1.3 miles home instead of biking so I’m not tempted to just collapse on the couch.

Mental note: check in with Dr. Patel at UC Davis Health in December about whether I can start driving over the Tower Bridge again. After that June 6, 2024 panic attack and his advice to avoid freeway driving for six months, the Yolobus 42 commute from West Sac has been my backup on days I’m with Mom.

Hi Mom,

Just got home from another long council night and wanted to send you an update before I forget the details.

First, on the riverfront: the council seemed genuinely excited about the upsized water, sewer, and storm drainage lines we’re putting in along 5th Street as part of the North Riverfront Mixed-Use Specific Plan. I didn’t say this at the dais, but in my head I was back in your Michigan Avenue basement in West Sacramento, looking at the mold after the January 2017 and February 2019 storms and listening to Dad wheeze. Knowing that better storm lines upstream might ease some of that backup is a quiet little victory, even if Sutter’s pulmonology notes on his COPD never make it into any staff report.

They also asked about “environmental justice and displacement risk,” so of course I was thinking about Miguel in unit 2B at Riverbend Court on 5th Street – 24 units, $1,050 a month on that month‑to‑month lease since August 2022 – and how the relocation plan I reviewed on January 10, 2024 shows $300–$400 rent increases if people are moved to newer complexes away from the levee. I’m pushing for stronger voluntary relocation assistance, but it’s hard not to see his face when we talk in abstractions.

On a different front, I’m still uneasy about that emergency zoning overlay they had us draft after the March 2025 overdose on the Northside playground. I know you were relieved they were “doing something” near Elena’s school, but I keep worrying about the message we send by blocking new sober living homes within 1,000 feet of the campus.

We’ll talk more this weekend when we come down for groceries and your blood pressure check.

Love you,
Amanda

metadata.subject: Council night + thinking about you all

artifacts.content:
Personal note – work, home, energy, and Daniel

February 2024

I spent this afternoon buried in the cumulative GHG and energy section for the North Riverfront Draft EIR (yes, officially a Draft EIR – boxes checked for residential, transportation, office, commercial, recreational, public plaza, promenade, etc., with all the air quality, water supply/groundwater, sewer capacity, traffic/circulation, noise, EJ, and GHG issues flagged). The funny part is how abstract it feels compared to our own SMUD bills sitting on the kitchen counter.

From July through December 2023, our electricity use at 250 Civic Center Plaza jumped 38%. That lines up almost perfectly with when Dr. Patel at UC Davis Health finally called my headaches “stress‑related migraines” in September 2023 and told me to work from home three days a week. More time inside, more AC, more laptop hours. Telecommuting looks good in the model, but the real‑life energy profile is messier.

Meanwhile, Daniel’s been dragging every Saturday. He started that bilingual support group in the basement of St. Mary’s Parish Hall on 3rd Street back in June 2023 for work‑related stress and insomnia from his AP history load at Riverview High. On November 4, 2023 the facilitator mentioned that our project’s pile‑driving and the new building’s shade could make the room darker and noisier, which might scare off some of the more anxious folks – including him.

Add in my own anxiety about freeway driving after the June 6, 2024 Tower Bridge panic episode and the shift to Yolobus Route 42 from West Sac, and it’s no wonder I feel like the transportation/energy chapter is secretly about us.

Remember: these personal stories are useful for empathy, not for insertion into the CEQA text.

metadata.title: GHG work vs. home reality
metadata.location: Civic Center Plaza – home office nook
